fields.brief_block: A citizen's arrest charge is not warranted unless there is some evidence to support each statutory element. See Nelson v. State, 283 Ga. 119, 121 (2008) (no citizen's arrest instruction required where defendant did not personally witness the crime).
fields.finding_analysis: Nelson v. State is a Georgia Supreme Court felony murder case about a fatal shooting outside a Valdosta bar and the defendant's justification defense. It does not address citizen's arrest. The opinion's jury-instruction discussion concerns only whether the defendant was entitled to charges on voluntary manslaughter, involuntary manslaughter, and reckless conduct as part of an ineffective-assistance claim; the page cited (121) is the start of the ineffective-assistance division. Nothing in the opinion mentions citizen's arrest, its statutory elements, or any requirement that a defendant personally witness a crime. The proposition attributed to this case does not appear in it.
